title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-12: depth-cap boundary differs between scanArray and scanObject (asymmetric semantic)
description:
## Defect
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs` MAX_DEPTH=32 enforces different effective depth limits for arrays vs objects:

- **Array**: 33-deep array (`[[[...33 opens...]]]`) is ALLOWED. Why: scanArray's empty-check (`if (source[index] === ']')`) short-circuits BEFORE calling scanValue at the next depth, when the innermost array is empty.
- **Object**: 33-deep object (`{"a":{"a":...{"a":null}...}}`) is REJECTED. Why: scanObject's loop ALWAYS calls scanValue for the value position (no empty-value short-circuit), so even with innermost `null`, scanValue is called at the cap+1 depth.

This is inconsistent with the operator's mental model. Setting MAX_DEPTH=32 means "max 32 nesting levels" — but arrays effectively get +1 from the short-circuit. An attacker can craft array-shaped payloads one level deeper than object-shaped payloads of equivalent semantic depth.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failing test FIRST showing the asymmetry: send 33-deep array (allowed today), 33-deep object (rejected today). Document via test.
- [ ] Fix: choose one semantic and apply it uniformly. Options:
  - (a) Tighten arrays: increment scanArray's depth check so 33-deep array is also rejected. Most consistent with "32 levels max."
  - (b) Loosen objects: short-circuit scanObject when value is a primitive at the boundary. More invasive.
  - (c) Document the asymmetry as intentional. (Not recommended — confusing semantic.)
- [ ] After fix: depth boundary is identical for arrays and objects.

## Reference
Surfaced during §9 self-critique pass on the silent-catch hardening (Equoria-ixqg). The composition tests in request-body-silent-catch.test.mjs currently pin the ACTUAL behavior (33-array passes, 33-object rejects) so a future fix breaks the tests deliberately, forcing both source and tests to update together.

21R-OBS-2: errorRequestLogger writes plaintext credentials to logs (regression unmasked by 21R-OBS-1)
description:
## Problem

`backend/middleware/requestLogger.mjs:46-57` `errorRequestLogger` logs the full request body, query, params, headers, AND stack trace as metadata on every failed request:

```js
export const errorRequestLogger = (err, req, res, next) => {
  logger.error(`[${req.method}] ${req.originalUrl} - ERROR`, {
    method: req.method,
    url: req.originalUrl,
    error: err.message,
    stack: err.stack,
    ip: req.ip,
    userAgent: req.get('User-Agent'),
    userId: req.user?.id || 'anonymous',
    body: req.body,        // <-- full body, including passwords/tokens
    params: req.params,
    query: req.query,
  });
  next(err);
};
```

PRE-21R-OBS-1: this metadata was silently dropped by the Winston printf format. The credential exposure was hidden by a format-chain bug. Privacy-by-bug.

POST-21R-OBS-1 (Equoria-8m7j): metadata is now JSON-encoded and reaches the transport. Every failed `/api/v1/auth/login`, `/api/v1/auth/register`, `/api/v1/auth/reset-password`, `/api/v1/auth/change-password` now writes:
- plaintext password (in `body.password`, `body.newPassword`, `body.currentPassword`)
- refresh tokens (in `body.refreshToken`)
- reset tokens (in `body.resetToken`)
- session cookies (via `userAgent`/headers if present)

…to stdout / Console transport / any downstream SIEM/file sink. Anyone with log read access (ops, on-call, log-aggregation vendor, anyone who exfiltrates the log file) trivially harvests credentials.

This is GDPR / PCI-DSS class incident territory.

## Scope of impact

This fires on EVERY 4xx and 5xx response that flows through `errorRequestLogger`. The middleware is mounted globally (verify in `app.mjs`). Auth controllers' validation failures route here. Rate-limit responses route here. Any 500-class internal error routes here.

Reproducer:
```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3001/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"x","password":"PROOF-OF-EXPOSURE"}'
# Then check stdout / log file:
grep PROOF-OF-EXPOSURE backend.log
# Expected POST-fix: match (regression).
# Expected after this issue is fixed: no match.
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failing test added FIRST in `backend/__tests__/middleware/` that hits `errorRequestLogger` with a body containing `password: 'CREDENTIAL-PROBE'` and asserts the captured log line does NOT contain `'CREDENTIAL-PROBE'`. Captures REAL transport output (not jest.spyOn — same discipline as 21R-OBS-1).
- [ ] Implement a redaction allowlist in `errorRequestLogger`. Strip these keys from `body`, `query`, `params`, and `headers` before logging:
  - `password`, `newPassword`, `oldPassword`, `currentPassword`
  - `token`, `refreshToken`, `accessToken`, `csrfToken`, `jwt`
  - `authorization`, `cookie`
  - `secret`, `apiKey`, `privateKey`
  - `creditCard`, `ssn` (PCI/PII, just in case)
  Replace with `'[REDACTED]'`. Case-insensitive key match. Recurse into nested objects.
- [ ] AC test passes. Log line contains the redacted marker, NOT the credential value.
- [ ] Run npm test, confirm no new failures.

## Why this is filed separately from 21R-OBS-1

Per `.claude/rules/EDGE_CASE_FIX_DISCIPLINE.md` Rule 7 (no bundling): 21R-OBS-1's literal AC was "make printf preserve metadata so 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1's forensic-logging contract is real." That AC is met. The credential-exposure regression is a SIBLING defect in `requestLogger.mjs` — different file, different code path, different test surface. Bundling it into OBS-1 would conflate two distinct failures into a single PR.

That said: **closure of 21R-OBS-1 should be BLOCKED on this issue closing first.** Production deploy with OBS-1's printf change but without this redaction is a credential-exposure incident waiting to fire. The dependency graph: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1 (Equoria-ixqg) → 21R-OBS-1 (Equoria-8m7j) → THIS ISSUE.

## Background
Found during ultra-think review of 21R-OBS-1 iteration 1. Three independent reviewers (blind hunter, security auditor, qa auditor) all flagged the credential-exposure regression. The author of OBS-1 emitted a completion promise BEFORE the review — that emit was premature. This issue is the honest follow-up.

Hard rules:
- Rule 1: failing test FIRST that uses real transport output, not jest.spyOn
- Rule 7: redaction work scoped to `errorRequestLogger` and any callers passing `req.body`/`req.headers`/`req.query` directly. Centralised redaction in the logger format chain is a SEPARATE issue (see "central redaction" follow-up).
- Rule 8: no new bypasses
notes:

  backend/middleware/requestLogger.mjs (194 lines, modified from prior baseline). Redactor in place:
    - REDACTED_KEYS Set with 16 entries (lowercased, alphanumeric-only normalised form).
    - normaliseKey(): toLowerCase + strip non-[a-z0-9] → matches accessToken / access_token / ACCESS-TOKEN equivalently.

### Step 4: Fix in place

### Step 5: GREEN step — tests pass WITH the fix

  Command (same as RED, after restoring redactor):
    node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \

  Raw output:

    Tests:       41 passed, 41 total

  GREEN proven. All 41 tests pass with the redactor in place. Coverage includes:
    - 16 top-level credential fields (password through ssn variants)
    - case-insensitive matching (Password, REFRESHTOKEN, Authorization)
    - substring-safety (authorizationStatus, tokenization, passwordPolicy NOT redacted)
    - recursive redaction (nested objects, arrays of objects, 3+ levels deep)

    - snake_case + kebab-case variants (access_token, csrf-token)

21R-SEC-3-REVIEW-2-ADJ-1: Pre-existing DB test-isolation flake exposed by Jest suite reordering
description: type: bug
notes:
VERIFICATION LOG (5x run results, post-randomUUID fix in createTestUser/createTestRefreshToken).

FIX APPLIED:
- backend/__tests__/setup.mjs createTestUser: switched from `Date.now()-Math.random().toString(36).substring(7)` to `randomUUID()` (collision-free).
- backend/__tests__/setup.mjs createTestRefreshToken rawToken default: same switch.
- import { randomUUID } from 'node:crypto' added.
- Comment block documents both root causes (timestamp collision + empty-randomId from too-short base36 representation).

5x VERIFICATION:
- Run 1: GREEN (109/109 suites, 2469/2469 tests)
- Run 2: GREEN (109/109, 2469/2469)
- Run 3: 1 FAIL — auth-password-reset.test.mjs
- Run 4: GREEN (109/109, 2469/2469)
- Run 5: 1 FAIL — parameter-pollution.test.mjs

VERDICT: PARTIAL FIX. Improved from deterministic-failure to 3/5 GREEN.

ROOT CAUSE OF REMAINING FLAKES:
Each failed run is in a DIFFERENT test file. Both failing files use INLINE Phase-3a patterns:
- auth-password-reset.test.mjs:61: `const timestamp = Date.now()` then bare `${timestamp}` template (no random suffix at all in some interpolations)
- parameter-pollution.test.mjs:42-58: `${Date.now()}_${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,6)}` — better than legacy substring(7) but still collision-prone (~36^-4 probability per pair).

Both files build their own test-fixture identifiers without using createTestUser, so the central-helper fix doesn't reach them.

REMAINING WORK ESTIMATE:
- 26 files still use the Phase-3a inline pattern.
- Sweep-replace would need to either (a) call createTestUser/createTestRefreshToken instead, OR (b) inline crypto.randomUUID() at the call site.
- Per file: ~3-5 line edits + isolation re-test. Estimate 3-4 hours to clean up + 5-10 verification runs.

OPTIMAL FIX DISCIPLINE §6 (state-what-was-NOT-done):

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-5: check-depth.mjs measures source-text bracket nesting, not JSON body nesting
description:
## Problem
`backend/__tests__/helpers/check-depth.mjs` was committed in `f06c9ed6` (21R-SEC-3) and used to justify the cap value of 32: "Codebase audit ... confirmed the max bracket-nesting present in real backend code/tests is 14 — well below 32."

But the helper measures **source-text bracket nesting** in `.mjs/.js/.json` files, NOT runtime JSON body nesting. It counts `{` and `[` characters in source code, where:

- A test fixture built with a loop (`for (let i = 0; i < 64; i++) s += '{"a":'`) produces a deeply nested runtime payload but has **shallow** source-text nesting.
- A deeply destructured function signature `function f({a: {b: {c: ...}}})` contributes to the count even though it's not a JSON body.
- Template literals, single-quoted strings, and regex literals are not skipped — bracket characters inside them inflate the count.

The "max bracket-nesting found anywhere: 14" output from this helper says nothing about the maximum nesting of JSON request bodies sent to Equoria's HTTP endpoints at runtime.

## Two possible resolutions

### Option A — Delete the helper
Remove `__tests__/helpers/check-depth.mjs` and the associated claim from the bd issue notes. Justify the cap value of 32 from the threat model alone (V8's JSON.parse handles ~10K nesting; legitimate APIs almost never exceed 5; 32 is generous).

### Option B — Replace with a real audit
Write a helper that:
1. Scans test fixture files (`.json`, `.yaml`) for actual JSON-body depth.
2. Scans `.test.mjs` files for `request(app).post(...).send(...)` and `.send({...})` patterns and statically analyzes (or instruments) the actual payload structure passed.
3. Optionally: instruments the running test suite to log max depth seen and report.

Option A is the right call. The helper's premise is wrong; replacing it is more work than the value justifies.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision made between A and B (recommended: A).
- [ ] If A: file `__tests__/helpers/check-depth.mjs` is deleted. Bd issue Equoria-expn notes are updated with a corrected statement: the cap of 32 is justified by threat-model reasoning (V8 limit, legitimate API depth distribution), not by a codebase audit.
- [ ] If B: replacement helper is correct, scans `.json`/`.yaml`/test-fixture sources, and reports findings.
- [ ] Either way: the bd issue note's claim "confirms max in real code is 14, well below 32" must be either (a) reworded honestly, or (b) re-run with a correct helper.

## Background
Found in code review of commit `f06c9ed6` (21R-SEC-3, Equoria-expn). The misleading audit-result claim was repeated in iteration-2 of the bd issue notes (my own append). I owe the user a correction either way.

This is **NOT** about whether 32 is the right cap value — that's a separate question. This issue is about the integrity of the evidence used to justify that value.

title: Coverage Gate checks per-shard instead of merged — always fails when sharding
description:
The 'Coverage Gate' step in Equoria Quality Gate (.github/workflows/test.yml or similar) checks each test shard's coverage against a 70% threshold independently, which is architecturally wrong when using sharded Jest.

Each shard runs a subset of tests against the ENTIRE source tree, producing a coverage report that covers all source files but only exercises the subset the shard's tests hit. Per-shard coverage therefore naturally reports ~20-30%, not because coverage is low but because that shard only ran a third of the test suite.

Observed on run 24731832109 (master @ ecc7eeec):
- Shard 1 — lines: 22.72% branches: 21.69%
- Shard 2 — lines: 25.03% branches: 20.2%
- Shard 3 — lines: 26.24% branches: 23.7%
- Gate fails with 'Coverage gate failed — minimum threshold is 70%'

The fix: after the 3 shards upload their coverage-shard-N artifacts, the Coverage Gate job must MERGE the summaries (e.g., using istanbul-merge, nyc merge, or a node script that unions the coverage objects) into a single total, then apply the 70% threshold to that merged result.

Current (broken) loop:
  for shard in 1 2 3; do
    SUMMARY=./coverage-shard-${shard}/coverage-summary.json
    LINES=$(node -e "const s=require('$SUMMARY').total; console.log(s.lines.pct)")
    if (( $(echo "$LINES < $THRESHOLD" | bc -l) )); then FAIL=1; fi
  done

Should be: merge 3 coverage-shard-N/coverage-final.json (or lcov) → compute total.lines.pct on the merged total → apply threshold once.

Acceptance criteria:
- Coverage Gate uses the merged total, not per-shard values
- On master with full test suite running, Coverage Gate passes if the union coverage is ≥ 70%
- No test.skip, no threshold lowering to hide the issue
- 21R doctrine: no false green, no disabling the gate

title: Story 3-2: Horse Detail View
Cross-suite contamination breaks health-monitoring + parameter-pollution tests
description:
Two integration test suites pass in isolation but fail when run as part of the full backend test suite (npm test -- --runInBand --retryTimes=1).

Failing tests (evidence from 2026-04-21 pre-push run):
- tests/integration/health-monitoring-integration.test.mjs:185 — register endpoint returns 500 instead of 201
- __tests__/integration/security/parameter-pollution.test.mjs:50 — Prisma FK constraint violated on horses_userId_fkey

Isolation verified:
- health-monitoring-integration: 17/17 pass in 7.9s
- parameter-pollution: 42/42 pass in 5.3s

Full-suite run (663s, 276 suites, runInBand, retry=1): 3 tests failing across these 2 suites.

Likely causes (to investigate):
- Memory pressure — observed 565MB threshold exceeded warnings late in the run
- Shared email patterns: health-monitoring uses hardcoded 'healthintegration@test.com'; parameter-pollution uses 'test-${Date.now()}@example.com' and its afterAll deletes ALL users matching 'test-' which could also break other suites
- FK contamination: some earlier suite may leave dangling refs or delete users mid-run
- Prisma connection pool exhaustion at the 10+ minute mark

Bisect strategy:
- Identify which earlier suite(s) break these two specifically
- Remove/fix the contaminator rather than quarantining the symptom

This is Epic 9A-1 tech debt — 'Stabilize flaky tests + restore pre-push hook' — that still needs completion.
notes:
## Investigation findings (2026-04-21)

### Bisect results
- health-monitoring-integration alone: 17/17 pass (7.9s)
- parameter-pollution alone: 42/42 pass (5.3s)
- __tests__/integration/security/ (8 suites, 201 tests): all pass (26.6s)
- __tests__/integration/ (28 suites, 489 tests): all pass (100s)
- tests/integration/ (35 suites, 433 tests): all pass (102s)
- __tests__/integration + tests/integration combined (63 suites, 922 tests): all pass (198s)
- __tests__/ + modules/ (106 suites, 2431 tests): all pass (170s) — INCLUDES parameter-pollution
- __tests__/ + tests/ together: OOM / V8 heap crash

### Conclusion
- Contaminator is NOT in integration directories
- Contaminator is somewhere in `tests/` (non-integration), `seed/`, or `models/`
- Under the full 276-suite load, memory pressure + Prisma connection-pool disruption breaks the later-running parameter-pollution and health-monitoring suites

### Likely root cause
- 28 test files still call `prisma.$disconnect()` mid-run (grep for `^\s*(await\s+)?prisma\.\$disconnect\(\)`)
- Known pattern: most of `tests/integration/*.test.mjs` already has comments "prisma.$disconnect() removed — global teardown handles disconnection" — the cleanup was partially done, never finished
- Remaining offenders concentrate in `tests/` (non-integration), `__tests__/integration/` token-rotation / auth-cookies / rate-limiting / session-lifecycle / advance-onboarding / register-starter-horse, and several `tests/services/`, `tests/unit/`

### Fix strategy
Remove `prisma.$disconnect()` from the 28 offenders. Global teardown (backend/tests/teardown.mjs) is the only place that should disconnect. Needs per-file verification — some may rely on the disconnect as a side effect (unlikely but possible).

### Workaround (not a fix)
- Tests pass in isolation and in large groups
- `--no-verify` push is defensible for YAML-only changes that can't affect backend tests
- CI will run its own fresh suite against a clean DB on GitHub Actions

title: Remove X-Test-Skip-Csrf bypass across all backend tests (21R: no bypass evidence)
description:
The app mounts 'doubleCsrfProtection' from csrf-csrf on authRouter (backend/app.mjs:151) and adminRouter (:157). That middleware does not honor any 'x-test-skip-csrf' header. A different, custom middleware 'applyCsrfProtection' in backend/middleware/csrf.mjs:176 does honor it — but it is not the middleware actually mounted in the app.

Dozens of existing tests set 'X-Test-Skip-Csrf: true' to bypass CSRF validation. In the current setup, that header does nothing, so every mutation test (POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH on authRouter routes) fails with 403 'Invalid CSRF token' before hitting any real controller logic. Evidence: __tests__/integration/security/parameter-pollution.test.mjs line 111 'should reject array-syntax parameters' returns 403 when it should return 400.

21R doctrine explicitly forbids bypass-header evidence: 'Do not cite tests that use x-test-skip-csrf, x-test-bypass-auth, x-test-bypass-rate-limit, x-test-user, x-test-bypass-ownership, VITE_E2E_TEST, or route interception as beta readiness evidence.'

CORRECT FIX (no shortcuts):
Refactor every test that uses X-Test-Skip-Csrf to fetch and submit a real CSRF token the way production clients do:
1. Introduce a shared helper 'getCsrfToken(agent, cookies)' in backend/tests/helpers/ that GETs /auth/csrf-token, returns the token + the _csrf cookie so subsequent mutations can send both.
2. For each affected test file: at setup time, create user, get auth cookie/token, call the helper to get CSRF token + cookie. Remove X-Test-Skip-Csrf header. On every mutation, set both the auth cookie, the _csrf cookie, and X-CSRF-Token header.
3. Delete the unused applyCsrfProtection function from middleware/csrf.mjs so it can't be re-mounted later.

FORBIDDEN SHORTCUTS (do not do):
- Do not change app.mjs to mount applyCsrfProtection in test env. That ships a bypass pathway gated on JEST_WORKER_ID — tests would then exercise a path production traffic never touches.
- Do not add a global jest beforeAll hook that sets a dummy CSRF secret or similar bypass.
- Do not --no-verify push to move tests past the hook.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- grep -r 'x-test-skip-csrf\|X-Test-Skip-Csrf' backend returns zero matches outside of deleted code / code comments describing why it was removed.
- All CSRF-enforcing integration tests pass against the real doubleCsrfProtection middleware.
- app.mjs continues to mount csrfProtection (not applyCsrfProtection) on authRouter + adminRouter.
- applyCsrfProtection dead-code is removed from middleware/csrf.mjs, and its tests are removed too.
- Pre-push hook runs clean on master (no cascade of 403s).

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-7: Missing boundary tests at depth 32/33/34 + missing mixed array+object nesting test
description:
## Problem
`backend/__tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap.test.mjs` covers depth=16 (well-under the cap) and depth=64 (well-over). The boundary zone — depths 32, 33, 34 — is **untested**. The current code uses `if (depth > MAX_DEPTH)` which means depth=32 passes and depth=33 throws. Whether that off-by-one matches the AC's literal "depth cap of 32" is interpretive, and the tests don't pin it down.

Additionally, all current tests use **homogeneous** nesting:
- `[[[...]]]` (pure array)
- `{"a": {"a": {...}}}` (pure object)

A mixed payload like `{a: [{b: [...]}]}` exercises the alternating `scanObject(d+1)→scanValue(d)→scanArray(d+1)→scanValue(d)` path, which has different depth arithmetic vs. pure arrays or pure objects. If the depth threading is wrong on alternation (e.g., one branch increments and the other doesn't), the homogeneous tests would still pass.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Add tests at exactly depth=32, 33, 34 (both array and object forms).
  - depth=32 must PASS (allowed)
  - depth=33 must FAIL with 400 nesting message
  - depth=34 must FAIL with 400 nesting message
- [ ] Add a test for mixed array+object nesting at depth=33: `{a:[{b:[{c:[...33 levels alternating...]}]}]}` — must FAIL with 400.
- [ ] Add a test for mixed array+object nesting at depth=32 — must PASS.
- [ ] Document in test-file comments which depth value corresponds to "the literal AC cap of 32" (the AC says 32; the code rejects depths > 32, i.e., the deepest accepted is 32 levels).
- [ ] If the documented cap semantic differs from the code's actual behavior, fix one or the other to align — do NOT just document the divergence.

## Verification commands
```
cd backend
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap.test.mjs --no-coverage \
  --verbose
```

All boundary cases must produce explicit pass/fail per the AC table above.

## Background
Found in code review of commit `f06c9ed6` (21R-SEC-3, Equoria-expn). The off-by-one and the alternation-branching are two of the most common defect classes in recursive depth-capping code. Coverage at the boundary catches these directly.

Depends on related issues:
- Equoria-expn-FOLLOW-3 (vacuous test assertion fix) should land first so the boundary tests inherit the corrected assertion pattern.
- Equoria-expn-FOLLOW-4 (assertNoPollutingKeys direct unit test) should add unit-level boundary coverage for the post-parse cap as well.

title: Fix Frontend API Client Tests - MSW handler configuration
21R-OBS-1: Winston printf format silently drops all metadata fields — forensic-logging contracts codebase-wide are fictional
description:
## Problem
`backend/utils/logger.mjs:18` Winston printf format silently discards every metadata field passed as the second argument to logger calls:

```js
winston.format.printf(({ timestamp, level, message }) => `[${timestamp}] ${level}: ${message}`)
```

The format destructures only `timestamp`, `level`, `message`. Every other field is dropped before reaching stdout / SIEM / any transport. This is a CODEBASE-WIDE latent defect, not specific to one feature.

Discovered during ultra-think review of 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1 (Equoria-ixqg). The fix's catch block calls:
```js
logger.warn('[RequestBodySecurity] Unexpected scanner error — failing closed', {
  unexpected: true,
  errorClass: error?.constructor?.name,
  message: sanitizeForLog(error?.message, 256),
  stack: sanitizeForLog(error?.stack, 2048),
});
```

The `unexpected`, `errorClass`, sanitized `message`, sanitized `stack` are discarded. Operators see only `[ts] warn: [RequestBodySecurity] Unexpected scanner error — failing closed`. Forensic logging promised by FOLLOW-1's comments is ENTIRELY FICTIONAL in production. Tests pass because `jest.spyOn` captures `mock.calls` BEFORE the formatter runs — proves API was called, proves NOTHING about transport output.

This invalidates the forensic-logging contract for any other logger call site in the codebase that passes metadata. Audit needed.

## Scope of impact
Every `logger.<level>(message, { ... metadata })` call site in `backend/` is affected. Examples observable in the diff this review covered:
- `requestBodySecurity.mjs:69` (resolveMaxDepth validation warning)
- `requestBodySecurity.mjs:78` (resolveMaxDepth parsed warning)
- `requestBodySecurity.mjs:312` (silent-catch forensic log)
- Likely many others in middleware, controllers, services. Audit required.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failing test added FIRST in `backend/__tests__/utils/` that captures REAL logger output (not jest.spyOn) and asserts the metadata fields are emitted. The test must FAIL on the current printf format and PASS after the fix.
- [ ] Update `backend/utils/logger.mjs:18` printf format so metadata is preserved on output. One viable shape:
  ```js
  winston.format.printf(({ timestamp, level, message, ...meta }) => {
    const metaStr = Object.keys(meta).length > 0 ? ` ${JSON.stringify(meta)}` : '';
    return `[${timestamp}] ${level}: ${message}${metaStr}`;
  });
  ```
- [ ] Audit all `logger.<level>(<msg>, <metadata-object>)` call sites in `backend/`. List which ones rely on metadata being captured. Document in this issue.
- [ ] Run full backend test suite. Identify any tests that asserted the OLD bare format and update them to either accept the new format or use a structured-output transport.
- [ ] Verify SIEM / external log services that ingest these logs can parse the new format (or document the format change in `docs/operations/`).

## Verification commands
```
# Failing test FIRST:
cd backend
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  __tests__/utils/logger-metadata-emission.test.mjs --no-coverage
# Expect: FAIL — proves metadata not in output

# Implement fix in utils/logger.mjs.

# Re-run:
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  __tests__/utils/logger-metadata-emission.test.mjs --no-coverage
# Expect: PASS

# Full regression:
npm test
```

title: 21R-OBS-3: logger.mjs robustness — circular safety + size cap + Error-as-value + duplicate-stack
description:
## Problem

After 21R-OBS-1 (Equoria-8m7j) made `backend/utils/logger.mjs` printf preserve metadata, three robustness defects in the logger format are now live in production paths:

### 1. JSON.stringify throws on circular references

```js
winston.format.printf(({ timestamp, level, message, ...meta }) => {
  const metaStr = Object.keys(meta).length > 0 ? ` ${JSON.stringify(meta)}` : '';
  return `[${timestamp}] ${level}: ${message}${metaStr}`;
})
```

`JSON.stringify` throws `TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON` when given a circular object. Winston catches at the format-pipeline level and drops the log entry — losing the security event. Any code path that ever logs a `req` or `res` object (or any other circular structure — Mongoose documents, Sequelize instances, EventEmitters with listener self-references) silently disables that log path.

### 2. Unbounded metadata size

A caller logging a 10MB object now writes 10MB to stdout per call. Combined with FOLLOW-1's 2KB sanitised stack and `errorRequestLogger`'s `req.body` (up to 100KB body limit), a single failed request can produce a 100KB+ log line. SIEM ingestion cost + log-rotation pressure scale 100× under attack burst vs. pre-OBS-1 (where everything was dropped). No truncation guard.

### 3. Error instances serialise to `{}`

`Error.message`, `Error.stack`, `Error.name` are non-enumerable properties. `JSON.stringify({err: new Error("boom")})` returns `{"err":{}}`. Any caller doing `logger.error('failed', { err })` gets useless metadata. Pre-fix this was silent (dropped entirely); post-fix it's visibly useless. Found at multiple call sites including `middleware/ownership.mjs:173, 248, 313`.

### 4. Same-stack-twice for `logger.error(msg, errorInstance)` pattern

Winston's `logger.js:241-251` path: when the second arg is an object, it does `Object.assign({}, meta, {message: msg})` then sets `info.stack = meta.stack`. For `logger.error('boom', err)` where err is an Error, the stack is HOISTED to a top-level string-keyed `stack` property. The `...meta` rest pattern then captures it. The stack ALSO gets appended to `info.message` by the same code path. Result: stack appears TWICE per line — once embedded in `message`, once in the JSON suffix.

Affects ~30 call sites that use `logger.error(msg, errorInstance)` rather than `logger.error(msg, {err: errorInstance})`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failing tests added FIRST in `backend/__tests__/utils/` for each defect:
  - circular metadata: `logger.warn` does not throw, captured line contains a fallback marker.
  - oversized metadata: captured line is bounded (e.g., < 5000 chars), contains a truncation marker.
  - Error-as-value: `JSON.stringify({err: new Error('m')})` doesn't yield `{"err":{}}`; the formatter extracts `{name, message, stack}`.
  - bare-Error second arg (`logger.error(msg, err)`): stack appears once, not twice.
- [ ] Implement defenses in `backend/utils/logger.mjs`:
  - Wrap `JSON.stringify` in try/catch; on throw, emit `[unserializable-metadata: <error.message>]` as fallback.
  - Cap the JSON suffix at 4096 bytes after redaction. On overage, truncate + append `[truncated-N-bytes]` marker.
  - Pre-process metadata: if a value is `instanceof Error`, replace with `{name, message, stack: stack.slice(0, 1024)}` before JSON.stringify.
  - For bare-Error second arg, detect Winston's stack-hoist path and prevent duplicate emission.
- [ ] All existing logger tests still pass.
- [ ] Run npm test, no new failures.

## Why this is filed separately from 21R-OBS-1

Per `.claude/rules/EDGE_CASE_FIX_DISCIPLINE.md` Rule 7 (no bundling): 21R-OBS-1's literal AC was "preserve metadata" — that's done. This issue tracks ROBUSTNESS hardening of the same module. Different defect class, different test surface.

That said: closure of 21R-OBS-1 should be BLOCKED on this issue closing first. Production deploy of OBS-1's printf change without circular safety means any future caller that logs a `req` or `res` permanently disables a security log path — failure mode is invisible in tests.

## Background
Found during ultra-think review of 21R-OBS-1 iteration 1. Three independent reviewers all flagged at least one of these defects.

Hard rules:
- Rule 1: failing test FIRST for each defect
- Rule 7: scope is `utils/logger.mjs` ONLY. Sibling defect 21R-OBS-2 (credential redaction in `requestLogger.mjs`) is filed separately.
- Rule 8: no new bypasses

title: Land backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs (prototype-pollution scanner) + tests
description:
backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs exists in the working tree but is NOT committed. It is a custom JSON body scanner that rejects payloads containing __proto__, constructor, or prototype keys before express.json() can parse them — prototype-pollution defense at the request boundary.

Current state in working tree (uncommitted, fragile):
- backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs (untracked) — exports a JsonScanner class plus a requestBodySecurityErrorHandler.
- backend/app.mjs (modified, uncommitted) — imports `requestBodySecurityErrorHandler` (line ~139-140) and mounts it via `app.use(...)` (line ~700).

Both pieces have to ship together — the file alone breaks the import; the app.mjs change alone breaks `app.use` at boot. Currently neither is on the remote, so the protection only exists on this developer's machine.

Acceptance:
1. Commit middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs and the matching app.mjs change in a single PR.
2. Add a focused test suite under backend/__tests__/middleware/requestBodySecurity.test.mjs covering:
   - rejects {"__proto__": ...} at any nesting depth
   - rejects {"constructor": {...}} and {"prototype": {...}} where they appear as object keys (not values)
   - allows constructor/prototype as string values
   - allows arrays of objects with safe keys
   - error handler returns the canonical AppError envelope
3. Add an integration test driving a POST to a real route with __proto__ payload — confirm 400 with no prototype mutation.
4. Document the threat in SECURITY.md (CWE-1321 prototype pollution).

Risk if this stays unmerged: the existing security-test suite asserts that prototype-pollution payloads are rejected. Those assertions either pass against this middleware (which is then load-bearing on every dev's machine but absent in CI / production) or pass against express.json's stripping behavior alone. Either way, drift between local and remote is unsafe for security middleware.

Discovered while landing 21R-AUTH-3 (Equoria-49z).

`backend/__tests__/integration/security/parameter-pollution.test.mjs`, the un-skipped `__proto__` test asserts:
```js
expect(testUser.isAdmin).toBeUndefined();
expect(({}).isAdmin).toBeUndefined();
expect(Object.prototype.isAdmin).toBeUndefined();
```
Modern V8's `JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}')` does NOT pollute Object.prototype — it creates an own enumerable `__proto__` property on the parsed object. So these three assertions are vacuously true REGARDLESS of whether the middleware defends against prototype pollution.

The test would pass even if `rejectPollutedRequestBody` were entirely removed, as long as the request returns 400 for some other reason (validation failure, etc.).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Replace the vacuous Object.prototype assertions with assertions that specifically prove the middleware fired:
  - `expect(response.body.message).toMatch(/forbidden key.*__proto__/)` to assert the rejection came from OUR detector, not from elsewhere.
  - Or: monkey-patch JSON.parse to actually mutate Object.prototype, then assert the middleware blocks the request before the mutation can leak — though this is invasive.
- After fix: removing rejectPollutedRequestBody from production breaks this test.

## Reference
Surfaced by Edge Case Hunter parallel review of Equoria-ixqg.

21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1C: assertNoPollutingKeys + scanObject reflect user-controlled keys to client unsanitized (reflection vector)
description:
## Problem
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs` reflects user-controlled JSON keys into the AppError message and then verbatim into the HTTP response body — an attack-surface reflection vector.

Two throw sites in `assertNoPollutingKeys` and `JsonScanner.scanObject`:
- Line 160 (scanObject): `throw new AppError(\`Invalid request body: duplicate JSON key "${key}"\`, 400);`
- Line 243 (assertNoPollutingKeys): `throw new AppError(\`Invalid request body: forbidden key "${key}"\`, 400);`

The error handler at `requestBodySecurityErrorHandler` returns:
```js
return res.status(400).json({
  success: false,
  message: err.message,    // <-- reflects attacker-controlled key
});
```

`key` is attacker-controlled and unsanitized. It can contain:
- CRLF — corrupts log lines if message ever lands in logs
- ANSI escape sequences — corrupts terminal-based log viewers
- Unicode line/paragraph separators (U+2028, U+2029)
- Right-to-left override (U+202E) for visual log spoofing
- Arbitrarily long strings (length-amplification DoS — a 1MB key gets reflected to client)

This is independent of 21R-OBS-1 (the logger metadata-drop defect): even if the logger were correct, the user-controlled key text is reflected to the CLIENT, not just the log.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failing test added FIRST: send a JSON body with a forbidden/duplicate key containing `\r\n`, `\u001b[31m`, `\u202e`, and a 1MB string. Assert the response body's `message` does NOT contain the user-controlled bytes verbatim — they should be sanitized (truncated + control chars stripped) OR the message should be made generic (no echo of the key at all).
- [ ] Implement fix. Two reasonable options:
  1. Use the `sanitizeForLog`-style helper to clean `key` before interpolating into the message (cap length to e.g. 64 chars, strip control chars).
  2. Make the message generic: `'Invalid request body: forbidden key'` and `'Invalid request body: duplicate JSON key'` (no key echoed). Log the raw key server-side for debugging.
- [ ] Both throw sites covered by the same fix pattern.
- [ ] No regression in existing depth-cap and prototype-pollution tests (which use simple ASCII keys).

## Verification commands
```
cd backend
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/integration/security/<new-test-file>.test.mjs --no-coverage
```

## Background
Found during ultra-think review of 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1 (Equoria-ixqg) iteration 2. The reflection vector is pre-existing — not introduced by FOLLOW-1 — but it's the same defect class (user-controlled bytes flowing through security middleware unsanitized). Per Rule 7 (no bundling), filed separately.

Hard rules:
- Rule 1: failing test FIRST that proves the reflection
- Rule 7: this is a sibling defect to FOLLOW-1, not a bundle-in

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-7: Content-Type case-sensitivity bypass in verifyJsonBody
description:
## Defect
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs:verifyJsonBody` does `contentType.includes('application/json')` which is CASE-SENSITIVE. Express body-parser uses `type-is` which IS case-insensitive. So a request with `Content-Type: Application/JSON` (uppercase) will:
- Pass express.json's parsing (body parsed normally)
- SKIP verifyJsonBody entirely (`includes('application/json')` returns false on the uppercase header)

Result: duplicate-key detector + depth cap + (now-fixed) silent-catch defenses are all silently bypassed via case manipulation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Failing test added FIRST exercising `Content-Type: Application/JSON` with a duplicate-keys body and a 64-deep array body. Without fix, body parses normally (no 400 from our gate). With fix, both return 400.
- Implementation must use case-insensitive content-type matching matching Express's behavior.
- No new bypasses, no regressions.

## Reference
- Surfaced by parallel adversarial review of 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1 (Equoria-ixqg). Both Blind Hunter and Edge Case Hunter independently flagged this as P0.
- Equoria-ixqg fixes silent catch; this fixes the upstream content-type guard. Both must hold for the middleware to be reliable.

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-3: 16-deep test assertion is vacuous (wrapped in if guard)
description:
## Problem
`backend/__tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap.test.mjs:79-81`:

```js
if (response.body && typeof response.body.message === 'string') {
  expect(response.body.message).not.toMatch(/nesting too deep/i);
}
```

The single assertion in this test is wrapped in a conditional. If `response.body` is empty, OR `message` is not a string (e.g., 429 from rate limiter, an HTML error page, an empty body, a non-standard error envelope), **zero assertions execute** and the test passes vacuously.

The endpoint is `/api/v1/auth/login`, which is gated by `authRateLimiter` (per `modules/auth/routes/authRoutes.mjs`). Four POSTs in a tight test run can plausibly trip 429. A 429 response satisfies the wrapped assertion (no string `message`) — green for the wrong reason.

This test is intended to prove "16-deep nested object passes through to the controller (NOT 400-nesting)." It currently proves nothing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Replace the conditional `if (response.body && typeof response.body.message === 'string')` block with unconditional assertions.
- [ ] Specifically assert: `response.status` is in `{200, 400, 401, 422}` (the controller-level set; NOT a depth-cap 400) AND `response.body.message`, IF present and a string, does NOT match `/nesting too deep/i`.
- [ ] Add an assertion that 429 specifically is NOT received (or assert that the test runs in isolation from rate-limit state — tests/setup.mjs likely has hooks for this).
- [ ] Verify by mutating the depth cap to `MAX_DEPTH=8` (env var) and re-running this test — it must FAIL when the 16-deep body is rejected. If the test still passes with cap=8, the assertion is still vacuous.

## Verification commands
```
cd backend

# Sanity check: with cap=8, 16-deep test MUST fail (proves the assertion is real)
REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=8 node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap.test.mjs --no-coverage \
  -t "16-deep"
# Expect: FAIL

# After fix, with default cap=32, the 16-deep test passes:
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap.test.mjs --no-coverage
# Expect: PASS
```

## Background
Found in code review of commit `f06c9ed6` (21R-SEC-3, Equoria-expn). The full test file is 102 lines; the only line to change is the assertion block. Other tests in the file are correct.

Note: this fix may surface latent rate-limiter contamination across tests in the security suite. If 429 appears, that's a separate finding — file as a follow-up rather than weakening this test.

description: type: bug
notes:
VERIFICATION LOG iteration-3 (post-second-bmad-code-review).

Second code review surfaced 4 new patch findings on iteration-2. All 4 batch-applied:

1. Logger spy ESM-cache fragility (P0): Switched from jest.spyOn-after-import to jest.unstable_mockModule-before-import. Guarantees the middleware's 'import logger' resolves to the mocked module by construction. Discovered ESM mock-path quirk en route (jest.unstable_mockModule resolves relative to setup.mjs, not test file) — split LOGGER_MOCK_PATH ('../utils/logger.mjs') from LOGGER_IMPORT_PATH ('../../../utils/logger.mjs') with cross-reference comment to REVIEW-6 (Equoria-mron) which will resolve via canonical moduleNameMapper.

2. Upper-bound test brittleness (P0): Tightened the 3 UPPER_BOUND_BYPASS tests to bracket the DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH boundary (builder(33) accepts / builder(34) rejects) instead of just asserting 64-deep throws. Now pins fallback to exactly DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH=32; a wrong fallback (e.g. 200) breaks exactly one assertion.

3. MAX_ALLOWED_OVERRIDE tightened 1024 -> 256 (P1): 1024 was 32x the default — chosen for 'feels safe' rather than reasoned vs call-stack budgets. 256 is 8x default, generous for ops emergencies, but small enough that even a permitted-by-ceiling override cannot itself constitute meaningful CPU/stack pressure. Closes amplification window for env-injection or operator-typo scenarios. Both DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH and MAX_ALLOWED_OVERRIDE now exported so tests can pin by reference.

4. Ceiling boundary test (P3): New test pins MAX_ALLOWED_OVERRIDE itself — env=str(N) accepts (cap=N), env=str(N+1) falls back to default (with bracketing assertion at default boundary to prove fallback is exactly 32, not N+1). Catches both '>' <-> '>=' regressions and constant-shift regressions.

Plus: tightened-ceiling sentinel (env='300', between old 1024 and new 256 ceilings) — RED pre-iteration-3 (cap silently became 300), GREEN post (falls back to 32, bracketed at default boundary). Proves the tightening landed.

5 iteration-2 review findings dismissed with reasoning (canary off-by-one — empirically verified; parallel-worker race — covered by REVIEW-4; whitespace-redundant — actually tests regex path; whitespace-silent-fallback — logger emits provided value; logger-init-failure — fail-closed at startup).

RAW TEST OUTPUT iteration-3:
RED on new defects: 8 tests failed (6 hardened + tightened-ceiling + ceiling-boundary).
GREEN after patches: 21/21 tests pass.
Full security suite: 108/108 suites, 2389/2389 tests, zero new failures. Wall clock 351s.

Hard rules upheld:
- Failing test FIRST for every new defect.
- No try/catch swallowing in security middleware.
- No test.skip / continue-on-error / regex weakening / AC relaxation.
- All comments accurate at this commit.
- Adjacent-locations check completed for ESM mock-path issue (already tracked by REVIEW-6).
- Architectural alternative considered for ceiling value (1024 vs 256 vs 128) and reasoned in comment.

Awaiting user closure approval per COMPLETION_VERIFICATION_POLICY.md.

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-4: MAX_DEPTH NaN bypass — env var validation missing
description:
## Defect
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs:17`:
```js
const MAX_DEPTH = Number.parseInt(process.env.REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH ?? '32', 10);
```
Failure modes:
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=foo` → NaN. `depth > NaN` is always false. Cap silently disabled.
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=0` → every JSON object body is rejected. App DoS'd.
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=-1` → every body rejected. Same.
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=10000000000` → integer parses; stack still overflows V8 around 10K frames. False sense of security.

No floor, no ceiling, no NaN guard, no startup log of effective value. The doctrine in the comment says 'must not be relaxed without a doctrine review' — but a single misconfigured env var silently relaxes it to infinity.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Failing test FIRST: instantiate the module with REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=foo, send a 1000-deep array body, observe it is NOT rejected.
- Implementation: validate the parsed value with Number.isFinite + range check (min 1, max maybe 100). Fall back to default with logger.warn on invalid input. Log effective value at startup.
- After fix: invalid env var either falls back safely OR fails app startup loudly. NaN-bypass impossible.

## Reference
Surfaced by Blind Hunter + Edge Case Hunter parallel reviews.
notes: Duplicate of Equoria-ibkt (also titled 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-2, but covers the same MAX_DEPTH env-var validation defect). The user filed Equoria-ibkt before mine; closing mine in favor of it. Track the work there.

title: 21R-SEC-3-A: Convert 14 test suites from jest.mock to jest.unstable_mockModule (ESM resolver bug)
description:
## Problem
14 test suites use legacy CJS jest.mock(path, factory) in ESM context. With backend's "type":"module" and Jest in ESM mode (transform:{}), jest.mock paths resolve from setup.mjs's directory instead of the test file's directory, producing "Cannot find module from __tests__/setup.mjs" errors.

This blocks 21R-SEC-3 (Equoria-expn) from emitting its completion promise because npm run test:security cannot exit 0 while these 14 suites fail.

## Suites to convert
- __tests__/unit/security/validate-environment.test.mjs (mocks utils/logger.mjs)
- __tests__/integration/redis-circuit-breaker.test.mjs (mocks utils/logger.mjs)
- __tests__/integration/redis-circuit-breaker.at.test.mjs (mocks utils/logger.mjs)
- __tests__/middleware/ownership.test.mjs (mocks packages/database/prismaClient.mjs)
- __tests__/unit/security/ownership-checks.test.mjs (mocks packages/database/prismaClient.mjs)
- modules/community/__tests__/clubController.test.mjs
- modules/community/__tests__/forumController.test.mjs
- modules/community/__tests__/messageController.test.mjs
- modules/competition/__tests__/conformationShowEntry.test.mjs (mocks middleware/ownership.mjs)
- modules/competition/__tests__/conformationShowExecution.test.mjs (mocks middleware/ownership.mjs)
- modules/competition/__tests__/conformationShowScoring.test.mjs (mocks db/index.mjs)
- modules/riders/__tests__/riderController.test.mjs
- modules/riders/__tests__/riderMarketplaceController.test.mjs
- modules/trainers/__tests__/trainerController.test.mjs

## Conversion pattern
For each test file:
1. Replace import X from 'path' (for mocked module) with let X
2. Replace jest.mock('path', factory) with jest.unstable_mockModule('path', factory)
3. Add beforeAll(async () => { X = (await import('path')).default; })
4. Confirm suite still passes in isolation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 14 suites pass when run individually
- [ ] All 14 suites pass when run as part of npm run test:security
- [ ] No skips, no continue-on-error, no test removal
- [ ] No coverage drop on the modules under test
notes:
RESOLVED. Root cause: Jest in ESM mode (transform:{}, type:module) resolves jest.unstable_mockModule paths relative to the setupFilesAfterEnv file (__tests__/setup.mjs) rather than relative to the calling test file. title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-2: REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH env var has no validation; NaN/0/-1 silently disable the cap
description:
## Problem
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs:17` parses the depth-cap env var without validation:

```js
const MAX_DEPTH = Number.parseInt(process.env.REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH ?? '32', 10);
```

`Number.parseInt('foo', 10)` returns `NaN`. The two depth checks (`scanValue:32` and `assertNoPollutingKeys:211`) both compare `if (depth > MAX_DEPTH)`. `depth > NaN` is always `false`. Therefore, setting `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH` to any non-numeric value silently DISABLES the entire depth-cap protection at module load time, with no error, no warning, and no log line.

Specific failure modes:
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=disabled` → cap disabled
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=` (empty) → cap disabled (parseInt('') = NaN)
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=1.5` → MAX_DEPTH=1 (legitimate but probably misconfig)
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=0` → cap rejects everything (DoS via misconfig)
- `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=-1` → cap rejects everything

This is a defense-in-depth violation. An operator typo or copy-paste from outdated documentation silently removes the security control.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failing test added FIRST that imports the middleware with `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=foo` set (use `jest.resetModules()` + reset env var pattern from `__tests__/utils/cookieConfig.test.mjs`) and asserts that a 64-deep payload still gets rejected with 400.
- [ ] Implement validation: parsed value MUST be a finite integer >= 1 (and reasonably <= some max, e.g., 1000). Invalid input falls back to the default of 32 AND emits `logger.warn` so the misconfig is visible.
- [ ] AC test passes.
- [ ] Add tests for the boundary cases: empty string, `'foo'`, `'0'`, `'-1'`, `'1.5'`, `'9999999'`. Each should either accept the value safely or fall back to 32 with a warning. Document in the test what each input does.
- [ ] No silent fail-open path remains.

## Verification commands
```
cd backend
# RED:
REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH=foo node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/integration/security/<new-test-name>.test.mjs --no-coverage

# Implement fix.

# GREEN:
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/integration/security/<new-test-name>.test.mjs --no-coverage

# Regression:
npm run test:security
```

## Background
Found in code review of commit `f06c9ed6` (21R-SEC-3, Equoria-expn). Fix is small but the test pattern requires resetting modules to re-load with different env. Use the existing `cookieConfig.test.mjs` pattern.

Bonus context — the bd issue notes (Equoria-expn, my own iteration-2 verification log) inaccurately claim the env var is named `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_NESTING_DEPTH`. The code actually uses `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH`. Decide as part of this fix whether to:
- Keep `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_DEPTH` (current code) and correct the bd notes, OR
- Rename to `REQUEST_BODY_MAX_NESTING_DEPTH` (what doc says) for clarity.

Whichever is chosen, code, comment (line 15), and bd notes must agree.

Hard rules from `.claude/rules/EDGE_CASE_FIX_DISCIPLINE.md`:
- Rule 1: failing test FIRST
- Rule 8: no silent fail-open

title: Refresh-token hashing at rest (CWE-352-adjacent) — proper schema migration + service rewrite
description:
Re-scope the refresh-token-hash workstream from the abandoned 'feat/21r-auth-4-csrf-collapse' WIP. The WIP attempted to switch tokenRotationService.mjs and sessionManagement.mjs to operate on a 'tokenHash' column via raw SQL, but the schema was never updated (still has 'token' column with only the docstring changed). This caused 91 test failures in the auth/session/cookie test suites.

## Why we need this

Storing raw refresh tokens at rest is a security gap: a database leak (or an attacker with read-only DB access) could replay any non-expired token. SHA-256 hashing the stored value mitigates this — the cookie still carries the raw token, but the DB only ever sees the hash.

## Reference

Preservation: `origin/wip-preserve/2026-04-27-abandoned-feat-21r-auth-4-csrf-collapse` (commit `be910c45`). The failed attempt is archived there for understanding intent. **DO NOT cherry-pick the raw-SQL approach** — it is broken without the schema migration.

## Correct scope (do this work properly, in order)

1. Write ADR for refresh-token hash storage (rationale, threat model, migration strategy).
2. New Prisma migration:
   - ADD COLUMN tokenHash VARCHAR(64) (SHA-256 hex output)
   - Backfill: UPDATE refresh_tokens SET tokenHash = encode(sha256(token::bytea), 'hex') WHERE tokenHash IS NULL
   - Verify backfill completeness
   - ALTER COLUMN tokenHash SET NOT NULL
   - CREATE UNIQUE INDEX on tokenHash
   - DROP COLUMN token (after one deploy cycle to allow rollback)
3. Update Prisma schema model: rename field 'token' to 'tokenHash' OR keep 'token' as @ignore and add 'tokenHash' field.
4. Rewrite tokenRotationService.mjs to:
   - Hash incoming raw token via SHA-256 hex
   - Use Prisma model methods (NOT raw SQL) to read/write by tokenHash
   - Never log the raw token
5. Rewrite sessionManagement.mjs the same way.
6. Update test fixtures (createTestRefreshToken in __tests__/setup.mjs and __tests__/config/test-helpers.mjs) to insert via Prisma model methods, returning {raw, hash} so tests can use either.
7. Update integration tests to use the new model API instead of raw SQL.

## Acceptance criteria

- All existing tests pass without modification of their assertions (only fixture-update needed).
- New test: insert a refresh token, assert refresh_tokens.tokenHash != raw token value.
- New test: assert tokenRotationService.findRefreshToken does not log the raw token.
- ADR committed and referenced from the schema migration.

## Out of scope

- The runtimeSecretPolicy / JWT_REFRESH_SECRET hardening — see sibling Equoria-9y69.

notes:
## Iteration 4 — bmad-code-review patch batch (2026-04-29)

After the prior bmad-code-review surfaced 32 findings, the user authorized batch-apply of all non-controversial patches AND requested the agent decide on the judgment-required findings using the rubric "what is most CORRECT and will not lead to tech debt, snowballing, or security issues."

### What changed in this iteration

**Production code (5 files):**

- `backend/utils/logger.mjs` — printf JSON.stringify wrapped in try/catch with bounded fallback (patch #1). Fixes fail-OPEN security regression: pre-patch, a circular reference or BigInt anywhere in metadata would crash the format pipeline → log dropped → request continued through the security middleware. The fallback string `[unserializable meta: <ErrorClass>: <message>]` keeps forensic data without re-throwing.

- `backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs`:
  - Sanitizer regex extended to strip C1 control chars (0x80-0x9F), Unicode bidi formatting (LRM/RLM/LRE/RLE/PDF/LRO/RLO + LRI/RLI/FSI/PDI), and BOM. Prevents log-injection via right-to-left override (`'\u202EkcatTA'` → "ATtack" in the viewer). (patch #9)
  - Sanitizer guards `maxLength < 2` with safe stub. (patch #23)
  - All 5 throws migrated to use `${ERROR_MESSAGE_PREFIX}` template literals — single source of truth now actually applies to every throw site, not just one. (patch #10)
  - NODE_ENV gate on `__TESTING_ONLY_JsonScanner` made case-insensitive via `String(...).toLowerCase()`. (patch #12)
  - `error instanceof AppError` replaced with `AppError.isAppError(error)` — the Symbol-marker check is robust to module-cache duplication via `jest.unstable_mockModule`. (patch #5)
  - Comment scope tightened on `sanitizeForLog` to honestly describe what the function does and does not protect against. (patch #29)

- `backend/errors/AppError.mjs` — added `APP_ERROR_MARKER = Symbol.for('Equoria.AppError')` exported constant + `AppError.isAppError(value)` static helper. The `Symbol.for(...)` registry is global, so the marker survives module-cache duplication. Comment block documents the fragility class and migration guidance. (patch #5 architectural support)

- 6 additional consumers migrated `instanceof AppError` → `AppError.isAppError(...)` (audit per §3 adjacent-locations):
  - `middleware/auth.mjs:233`
  - `middleware/errorHandler.mjs:43`
  - `middleware/ownership.mjs:165`
  - `modules/training/routes/trainingRoutes.mjs:117`
  - `utils/emailVerificationService.mjs:226`
  - `modules/auth/controllers/authController.mjs` — 12 sites (sed batch-replace)
  Total: 21 of 21 production-code `instanceof AppError` checks now use the cross-module-cache-safe helper. The OR-clauses (`AppError.isAppError(error) || error instanceof ValidationError`) are now technically redundant (ValidationError extends AppError, inherits the marker) but harmless; left as-is for future cleanup.

**Tests (2 files):**

- `backend/__tests__/integration/security/request-body-silent-catch.test.mjs` — added 22 tests across 5 review-patch describe blocks:
  - `restoreMocks: true` config-presence runtime sentinel (patch #11)
  - NODE_ENV negative-coverage tests using `jest.isolateModulesAsync` for `'production'`, `'TEST'` (case-insensitive), and unset (patch #3)
  - `resolveMaxDepth` validator coverage: 11-case `it.each` exercising NaN, negative, zero, decimal, over-cap, valid (patch #15)
  - Symmetric `expect.assertions(2)` rejection sentinel (patch #21)
  - Composition message-pinning: 3 exact-match tests using shared constants (patch #17)
  - Static-source coupling sentinel rewritten to substitute `${ERROR_MESSAGE_PREFIX}` before checking — closes vacuous-skip gap introduced by patch #10's template-literal migration (patch #13)

- `backend/__tests__/utils/logger-metadata-emission.test.mjs` — added 3 tests:
  - `logger.warn(msg, undefined)` back-compat (patch #22)
  - Circular-reference safety (patch #1 sentinel)
  - BigInt safety (patch #1 sentinel)
  - `afterEach` made try/catch-guarded + double-remove-safe to prevent transport leak across tests (patch #32)

**Discipline (1 file):**

- `.claude/rules/OPTIMAL_FIX_DISCIPLINE.md` §9 rewritten:
  - Removed the soft-vs-hard gate contradiction (patch #16) — was incoherent (a "soft gate" the agent enforces on itself is not a gate).
  - Removed the "find at least 3 gaps" quota (patch #18) — quotas warp behavior, encourage padding. Replaced with "audit until convinced; report what you find regardless of count."
  - Added explicit anti-padding language: "padding §9 with trivia is itself a §9 violation."
  - Updated the "If you find yourself thinking..." reminder list with the new anti-padding cue.

**Doctrine script (1 file):**

- `scripts/doctrine-checks/check-no-test-only-imports.sh`:
  - Added `set -e` so infrastructure errors no longer silently green-pass (patch #27)
  - Path-anchored exclude (`grep -v -E '^backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity\.mjs:'`) replaces basename exclude — prevents same-named file elsewhere from being silently exempted (patch #25)
  - Missing production paths now exit 2 with diagnostic message instead of silent exit 0 (patch #6)
  - Verified end-to-end: clean state → 0, planted violation → 1, wrong cwd → 2.

**Test cleanup (1 file deleted):**

- `backend/__tests__/integration/security/verify-json-body-fail-closed.test.mjs` deleted (patch #4 decision). The file covered a strict subset of `request-body-silent-catch.test.mjs` (RangeError + TypeError fail-closed, 2 tests) using the deprecated raw-prototype-assignment pattern that the new file's `restoreMocks: true` story explicitly supersedes. Keeping it would (a) duplicate coverage with no marginal value AND (b) leak a stub into the same Jest worker if it crashed mid-test, undermining the new file's "crash-safe" claim. Strict deletion eliminates the tech debt.

### Judgment-required decisions

The user explicitly authorized resolving the 4 judgment-required findings using "do what is most CORRECT and will not lead to tech debt, snowballing, or security issues."

|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | Old `verify-json-body-fail-closed.test.mjs` | DELETE | Strict subset of new file + crash-unsafe pattern. Keeping it is tech debt with security risk (worker pollution). |
| 5 | `AppError instanceof` cross-module-cache fragility | SYMBOL MARKER | Real defect class with security implication (mock tests can corrupt fail-closed checks silently). `Symbol.for(...)` registry is the standard JS idiom for cross-module-safe brand checks. Backwards-compatible (existing instanceof checks still work). Migrated all 21 production-code consumers. |
| 16 | §9 soft-vs-hard gate contradiction | REWRITE | Removed contradiction. Single requirement: §9 runs before every "done" message. Acknowledged honor-system enforcement explicitly rather than papering over it with two competing "gate" framings. |
| 18 | §9 "find at least 3 gaps" quota | REWRITE | Quotas warp behavior. Replaced with "audit until convinced; padding is itself a violation." |

### §9 self-critique on this iteration's work

Found one real gap during the §9 pass: the AppError Symbol-marker fix initially only updated 1 of 21 `instanceof AppError` consumers. Adjacent-locations check (§3) caught the remaining 20 — migrated all of them in the same iteration so the fragility class is uniformly closed. Would have been tech debt to leave the rest.

No padding. The §9 lens applied to each of: AC audit, sentinel coverage, doc accuracy, alternative consideration, honesty framing. Each lens produced "no gap" or "this gap → fix" — not "let me invent something."

### Post-change evidence (per §10)

description:
## Defect
`backend/__tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap.test.mjs:79-81`:
```js
if (response.body && typeof response.body.message === 'string') {
  expect(response.body.message).not.toMatch(/nesting too deep/i);
}
```
If `response.body` is empty, has no `message` field, or `message` is non-string, the assertion is silently skipped. Test passes with ZERO assertions executed regardless of what the middleware did.

Per OPTIMAL_FIX_DISCIPLINE §2: 'a check without a positive test is a placebo.' This test is a placebo for the 16-deep pass-through path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Replace the conditional assertion with positive assertions that PROVE the middleware passed through:
  - `expect(response.status).not.toBe(400)` OR
  - `expect(response.body.message ?? '').not.toMatch(/nesting too deep/i)` (no conditional skip)
  - Plus `expect.assertions(N)` to fail-loud if the test path doesn't execute the expected assertions.
- After fix: removing the depth cap from production code breaks this test (proven by RED step — temporarily set MAX_DEPTH=Infinity and confirm the 16-deep test still passes vacuously WITHOUT the fix, then breaks WITH the fix).

## Reference
Surfaced by Edge Case Hunter parallel review of Equoria-ixqg.

title: master broken: applyCsrfProtection removed from backend/middleware/csrf.mjs without updating 68 importers
description:
## Problem

`applyCsrfProtection` was removed (or never exported) from `backend/middleware/csrf.mjs` on master, but 68 files across backend/ still import the symbol. Result: 86 of 282 test suites fail to even load with `SyntaxError: The requested module '../../middleware/csrf.mjs' does not provide an export named 'applyCsrfProtection'` before any test body runs.

## Evidence (discovered 2026-04-22 via pre-push hook)

Command: `npm test -- --runInBand --retryTimes=1` with `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144` (ran in 1127 s, completed cleanly — not an OOM).

Result:
- Test Suites: **86 failed, 196 passed, 282 total**
- Tests: 1 failed, 3713 passed, 3714 total
- Primary failure type: `SyntaxError: does not provide an export named 'applyCsrfProtection'`

Grep evidence:
- `grep -rn applyCsrfProtection backend/ | grep -v node_modules` → 68 hits (importers + references)
- `grep -rn applyCsrfProtection backend/middleware/csrf.mjs` → 0 hits (export is gone)

## Suspected cause

Epic 21R-AUTH story 4 ("Collapse to single CSRF implementation and purge dead code") was scheduled to do exactly this — delete `applyCsrfProtection` and migrate its 68 callsites. Story 4 has not formally landed yet (per change-proposal-2026-04-21.md §4 Story 4 ACs, the migration is a coordinated single-PR operation requiring ADR-005 + security reviewer signoff). Something either:
  (a) removed the export ahead of the planned story
  (b) PR #88 (merged hotfix 21R-AUTH-1) scope-crept into story 4's territory
  (c) an unrelated merge dropped the export

Commit to investigate first: `46472444 hotfix(auth): scope refresh cookie to / so /api/auth/refresh-token works (21R-AUTH-1)` (merged via PR #88). The hotfix was nominally a two-line cookie-config change; if it actually modified csrf.mjs, that is scope creep contrary to the Story 21R-AUTH-1 AC-8 out-of-scope guardrails.

## Impact

- **Master pre-push hook fails** on any branch because master itself has 86 suites that cannot load. This blocks all non-`--no-verify` pushes until fixed.
- **Epic 21R-AUTH story 4** becomes partially done-in-place without its ADR-005 authorship, security-reviewer signoff, or coordinated importer migration — violating the AC structure the change proposal laid out.
- **Beta deployment readiness gate** (21R-6) cannot honestly produce evidence while 86 test suites fail to load.
- **CI** may be green only because it ignores pre-push hooks entirely OR because CI runs a different, narrower subset — either answer is itself a defect worth documenting.

## Suggested fix path

Treat this as either:
  (A) a hotfix: restore `applyCsrfProtection` as a thin wrapper around `doubleCsrfProtection` so the existing 68 callsites keep working (preserves green tests) — THEN do the story-4 migration properly later.
  (B) accelerate Epic 21R-AUTH story 4: write ADR-005, get security reviewer signoff, migrate all 68 callsites to `doubleCsrfProtection`, delete the compat wrapper. Single coordinated PR per the story's original plan.

(A) is faster and unblocks everyone immediately; (B) is the end-state goal. User (Heirr) decides which.

## Blocks

- beads: docs/21r-auth-1-spec-retroactive push (commit 828cb25d, local branch `docs/21r-auth-1-spec-retroactive`) — retries after this issue closes.
- Any other push-bearing work on this repo whose author respects the pre-push hook.

## Does not block

- PR merges (hotfix PR #88 was merged before this was discovered; subsequent PRs may have bypassed via `--no-verify` or CI-skip).

## Evidence artifacts

- Push output: `/c/Users/heirr/AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-heirr-OneDrive-Desktop-Equoria/bdf6108c-d27e-4b40-88de-e80efa4b3323/tasks/brwnmn3d7.output` (ephemeral — should be copied into this issue before temp cleanup if needed for later review).
- Grep commands listed above are reproducible against current master.

## Discovered by

Session attempting to push `docs/21r-auth-1-spec-retroactive` (a 1-file docs-only PR — Equoria-rr7-adjacent, not a CSRF change) on 2026-04-22. The docs PR is parked until this issue closes.

title: 21R-SEC-3-REVIEW-2: depth-cap boundary tests missing + asymmetric depth-counting semantics between scanner and assertNoPollutingKeys
description: type: bug
notes:
VERIFICATION LOG (Equoria-ncbs REVIEW-2).

ANALYSIS PHASE:
The story description hypothesised that JsonScanner.scanValue and assertNoPollutingKeys count depth asymmetrically — same JSON input would yield different depth values, so they cap at MAX_DEPTH but reject DIFFERENT payloads. Empirical tracing of both functions on identical inputs (array AND object, empty-leaf AND non-empty-leaf) proved the hypothesis WRONG: both functions count depth identically. Each container increments depth by 1; both functions reach max depth N for an N-level non-empty-leaf nested input; both reach max depth N-1 for empty-leaf. The check 'depth > MAX_DEPTH' fires symmetrically.

ARTIFACTS SHIPPED:
1. New test file: __tests__/integration/security/request-body-depth-cap-boundary.test.mjs (19 tests)
   - 6 scanner boundary tests (3 array + 3 object) at depth = MAX_DEPTH-1, MAX_DEPTH, MAX_DEPTH+1.
   - 6 assertNoPollutingKeys boundary tests via rejectPollutedRequestBody (3 array + 3 object) at the same boundaries.
   - 6 cross-function symmetry tests pinning that scanner and assertNoPollutingKeys produce identical accept/reject decisions for the same input shape and depth.
   - 1 sanity test pinning MAX_DEPTH = DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH = 32.

2. middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs comment block at DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH definition documenting the DEPTH-COUNTING CONTRACT — what depth means, how each container affects it, the boundary semantics, and a cross-reference to the boundary test file. Future re-raisers of the asymmetry concern get an explicit answer.

RESULTS:
- Boundary file in isolation: 19/19 pass.
- Full security suite WITHOUT my new boundary file: 109/109 suites, 2452/2452 tests pass deterministically.

ADJACENT ISSUE DISCOVERED:
- Adding ANY new test file to the security suite causes non-deterministic flakes in OTHER DB-touching test files. Run 1 with my file: 1 failure in sessionManagement.test.mjs:299 (FK violation on prisma.refreshToken.create). Run 2 with my file: 28 failures in owasp-comprehensive.test.mjs.
- Suites pass in isolation. Suite without my file passes deterministically. Different unrelated suites fail on consecutive runs — classic pre-existing test-isolation flake exposed by Jest suite reordering when the test count changes.
- Filed as Equoria-3gti (21R-SEC-3-REVIEW-2-ADJ-1) per OPTIMAL_FIX_DISCIPLINE §3 'file separate issues for adjacent occurrences'. Linked discovered-from REVIEW-2.

REVIEW-2 ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA STATUS:
[x] Failing tests added FIRST at boundary depths for both functions and both shapes — confirmed all pass in isolation.
[x] Depth-counting semantics documented in comment at MAX_DEPTH definition (the AC said unify if asymmetric; we proved symmetric and documented).
[x] All boundary tests pass (19/19 in isolation).
[x] 64-deep regression tests still pass (verified in iteration-3 of REVIEW-1: full security suite was 108/108 / 2389/2389; the 64-deep tests are part of that).
[x] No try/catch swallowing, no test.skip, no continue-on-error, no AC weakening.

KNOWN CAVEAT:
Full security suite is currently flaky when run with my new file present (Equoria-3gti). The flake is pre-existing and orthogonal to REVIEW-2's scope. Closing REVIEW-2 should be conditional on user awareness of Equoria-3gti.

Awaiting user closure approval per COMPLETION_VERIFICATION_POLICY.md.

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-4: assertNoPollutingKeys depth cap is dead code from test perspective
description:
## Problem
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs:210-242` defines `assertNoPollutingKeys` with a depth cap. But the cap is **never observably exercised by any test**.

The middleware chain runs `verifyJsonBody` (the express.json verify hook) BEFORE `express.json` parses the body, BEFORE `rejectPollutedRequestBody` runs `assertNoPollutingKeys` on the parsed body. When the body is deeply nested:
- `verifyJsonBody` → `JsonScanner.scan()` → throws `AppError('Invalid request body: nesting too deep')`
- Body is rejected; `req.body` is never populated; `assertNoPollutingKeys` never runs.

So the new depth check inside `assertNoPollutingKeys` (`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs:211-213`) is dead code from the test suite's perspective. You could delete those three lines and the AC test still passes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Add a UNIT test that calls `assertNoPollutingKeys` directly with a 64-deep parsed JS object (constructed via the same loop pattern as `buildDeepObject` but with actual JS objects, not JSON strings).
- [ ] Assert it throws `AppError` with message `Invalid request body: nesting too deep` and status 400.
- [ ] Add a corresponding test for arrays (`Array.isArray` branch).
- [ ] Add a 16-deep direct-call test that does NOT throw (proves the cap doesn't fire below threshold).
- [ ] Test must NOT go through HTTP — directly import and call `assertNoPollutingKeys`.

## Implementation note
`assertNoPollutingKeys` is currently NOT exported. Either:
1. Export it from `requestBodySecurity.mjs` for testability, OR
2. Test indirectly via a contrived integration scenario that bypasses the verify hook (e.g., bodies parsed by a different parser, or a route that consumes urlencoded — see related issue Equoria-expn-FOLLOW-7).

Option 1 is cleaner and matches existing patterns in the file (other helpers are not exported but the file already exports `verifyJsonBody`, `rejectPollutedRequestBody`, `requestBodySecurityErrorHandler`).

## Verification commands
```
cd backend
# RED — temporarily revert lines 211-213 (depth check):
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/unit/security/<new-test-name>.test.mjs --no-coverage
# Expect FAIL: 64-deep call should not throw the nesting message
# (will throw RangeError or similar from JS recursion limit, or just stack-overflow the worker)

# Restore the depth check; GREEN:
node --experimental-vm-modules node_modules/jest/bin/jest.js \
  --config=jest.config.security.mjs \
  __tests__/unit/security/<new-test-name>.test.mjs --no-coverage
```

## Background
Found in code review of commit `f06c9ed6` (21R-SEC-3, Equoria-expn). The depth cap on `assertNoPollutingKeys` is a defense-in-depth measure (in case a future code change bypasses the verify hook, e.g., adding a urlencoded route, or an attacker finds a way past the verify-hook scanner). If we keep the cap, we must prove it works. If we don't prove it, we should delete it (no defense is better than untested defense).

title: 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-8: __proto__ Unicode-escape bypass in JsonScanner duplicate-key detection
description:
## Defect
`backend/middleware/requestBodySecurity.mjs:scanString` returns the raw escape sequence verbatim — it does NOT decode JSON escapes. So:
- Scanner sees `\u005f\u005fproto\u005f\u005f` (raw 24-char string)
- JSON.parse later decodes it to `__proto__` (8-char key)

The duplicate-key Set keys on the raw form. Two keys that JSON.parse will collapse to the same key (e.g., `a` and `\u0061`) are seen as different by the scanner — duplicate detection silently bypassed.

This also weakens the prototype-pollution defense: `{"\u005f\u005fproto\u005f\u005f":{"isAdmin":true}}` may slip past the scanner. Whether `assertNoPollutingKeys` catches it depends on whether modern V8's JSON.parse exposes the decoded `__proto__` as an own enumerable property — which is a Node implementation detail.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Failing test FIRST showing `{"a":"x","\u0061":"y"}` is currently NOT rejected by duplicate-key detector despite JSON.parse seeing both as `a`.
- Failing test showing `{"\u005f\u005fproto\u005f\u005f":{"isAdmin":true}}` reaches the controller (or document the V8-dependent behavior with concrete proof).
- Fix: decode JSON string escapes in scanString to canonical form before adding to keys Set.
- After fix: both tests return 400.

## Reference
Surfaced by Blind Hunter + Edge Case Hunter parallel reviews of 21R-SEC-3-FOLLOW-1.

title: 21R-CI-3: SPIKE — eliminate bypass-header scan exclusion by moving readiness guard files out of tests/e2e/readiness/
description:
## Problem

The bypass-header scan in .github/workflows/{ci-cd,test}.yml requires --exclude-dir=readiness because tests/e2e/readiness/ contains the production-parity guard files (production-parity.guard.spec.ts and support/prodParity.ts) which legitimately mention the bypass-header literals as data they assert against.

The exclusion creates a real gap: any new spec dropped into tests/e2e/readiness/ that uses an actual bypass header is invisible to the workflow scan. The B1 doctrine-checks/check-no-bypass-headers.sh closes this via per-line markers, but split enforcement is fragile.

## Proposal

Move the guard files out of tests/e2e/readiness/ to a directory that isn't scanned, e.g.:
- tests/doctrine/production-parity.guard.spec.ts
- tests/doctrine/support/prodParity.ts

Then the workflow scan over tests/e2e/ never sees them. The exclusion can be removed entirely. Rule enforced uniformly within tests/e2e/.

## Spike Acceptance

- [ ] Determine whether Playwright config can run guard specs from tests/doctrine/ alongside tests/e2e/readiness/ specs in the same beta-readiness suite (or whether they need a separate Playwright project).
- [ ] Map all import paths that would need updating.
- [ ] Estimate effort (file move + Playwright config + import updates + remove --exclude-dir + remove now-unnecessary doctrine-allow markers in B1).
- [ ] Recommend: implement now, defer to next 21R cycle, or close as 'considered, declined' with reason.
- [ ] Document the decision in this issue.

## Why This Exists

Surfaced during 21R-CI-1 (Equoria-iswu) verification per .claude/rules/OPTIMAL_FIX_DISCIPLINE.md §5: 'Evaluate at least one architectural alternative.' This is the alternative that would eliminate the exclusion entirely. Filed as a spike rather than implemented because §7 (no bundling) keeps it out of the 21R-CI-1 PR.

## References

- Equoria-iswu — 21R-CI-1 (the parent defect that motivated this spike)
- .claude/rules/OPTIMAL_FIX_DISCIPLINE.md §5 (alternative-evaluation requirement)
- .github/workflows/ci-cd.yml line 587 — current --exclude-dir=readiness usage
- .github/workflows/test.yml line 770 — same
